era_context: The late 13th century is marked by the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire, the consolidation of the Yuan Dynasty in China, the dominance of the Mamluk Sultanate in the eastern Mediterranean, and the centralization of European feudal monarchies. The Crusader era is ending; the age of European maritime expansion has not yet begun. In Scotland, political tensions are rising that will lead to the First War of Independence.

events.title: Scottish succession crisis and John Balliol's deposition
events.description: John Balliol, King of Scotland, is deposed following disputes over Scottish independence and English overlordship. The succession crisis reflects tensions between Scottish nobles and English claims to overlordship. The deposition of Balliol creates a power vacuum that Edward I of England will exploit with his invasion in 1296, initiating the First Scottish War of Independence. The crisis is a pivotal moment in Scottish political history.
key_figures: Edward I (King of England, claiming overlordship)

sources.name: Scottish historical traditions and later compilations

events.certainty_note: The deposition of John Balliol is documented in English and Scottish sources. The exact date and circumstances are well-established in historical records.
cross_references: Related to 1296-001 (Edward I's invasion of Scotland)

events.title: Franco-Scottish alliance (Auld Alliance) formalized
events.description: Scotland and France formalize an alliance against English expansion, establishing the 'Auld Alliance' (Old Alliance). The alliance reflects the shared interests of Scotland and France in resisting English hegemony. The alliance will persist for centuries and shape British and European politics. The formalization of the alliance is a significant moment in Scottish political development and reflects the emergence of Scotland as a distinct political entity.

sources.name: French royal records and diplomatic documents
Scottish historical traditions and later compilations

events.certainty_note: The formalization of the Franco-Scottish alliance is documented in French royal records, though the exact date of 1295 is traditional. Some sources place the formal alliance slightly later, but the diplomatic relationship is well-established by this period.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Temür Khan's growing influence
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty continues under Mongol rule, with Temür Khan gaining influence within the imperial court. By 1295, the Yuan Dynasty maintains control over China but faces ongoing administrative challenges and Han Chinese resistance movements. The dynasty's reliance on Mongol military elites and Han Chinese bureaucratic expertise creates ongoing factional tensions.
key_figures: Temür Khan (Mongol prince, later Emperor Wuzong)

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty administrative records and official histories document political succession and court dynamics.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate military campaigns and consolidation
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad continues military consolidation and faces threats from Mongol incursions from the north. The sultanate maintains control of trade routes and manages internal factional conflicts among Mamluk emirs. The period is marked by relative stability, allowing for economic recovery and architectural patronage.
events.certainty_note: Mamluk administrative records and near-contemporary chronicles provide detailed documentation.

events.title: Venetian-Genoese rivalry and Mediterranean trade competition
events.description: Venice and Genoa continue their intense commercial and military competition for control of trade routes to the Levant and Black Sea. By 1295, both republics maintain colonial outposts and trading posts throughout the Mediterranean. The rivalry shapes Mediterranean geopolitics and drives naval innovation and commercial development.

sources.name: Venetian and Genoese merchant records, notarial documents
Historiographic consensus on Mediterranean trade networks

events.certainty_note: Commercial records and diplomatic correspondence from both republics document ongoing rivalry and trade activities.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Franco-Scottish alliance formalization is disputed by modern scholarship. Some sources place the formal alliance slightly later than 1295, and the alliance may have developed gradually rather than through a single formal agreement. Additionally, the significance of John Balliol's deposition in determining the outcome of the First Scottish War of Independence may be overstated; the war was driven by broader Scottish resistance to English overlordship, not merely by succession disputes. The narrative of the 'Auld Alliance' as a unified, long-lasting partnership may also be romanticized; the alliance was pragmatic and often strained by competing interests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1295 is moderate and regionally uneven. European sources (English chronicles, French royal documents, Scottish traditions) are relatively abundant, though Scottish sources are often later compilations. East Asian sources (Yuan Dynasty records) are well-preserved. Islamic sources (Mamluk records) are detailed. However, the surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific sources are minimal or absent. The sources reflect the biases of literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-elite perspectives are largely lost. The Franco-Scottish alliance narrative, in particular, has been shaped by later Scottish and French historiography, which may emphasize dramatic diplomatic agreements over longer-term political and economic trends.

graph_edges.note: John Balliol's deposition creates the succession crisis that Edward I exploits with his invasion in 1296
Franco-Scottish alliance is formalized around the same time as Edward I's invasion of Scotland
